---
title: "Style Showcase"
subtitle: "Live Python, callouts, and sidenotes"
image: ../img/image-placeholder.png
date: 6-28-26
format:
    r-wasm/live-html:
      grid:
        margin-width: 350px
      pyodide:
        packages: [numpy, matplotlib]
execute:
  echo: fenced
reference-location: margin
citation-location: margin
toc: true
toc-location: left
---

# Introduction

This post exists purely to stress-test styling: live Python code blocks, collapsible callouts, and margin sidenotes.[^intro]

[^intro]: Sidenotes render in the right margin when `reference-location: margin` is set and the viewport is wide enough. On narrow screens they fall back to footnotes at the bottom.

# Live Python

The cell below runs directly in the browser via Pyodide — no server needed.[^pyodide]

[^pyodide]: Pyodide compiles CPython to WebAssembly. First load downloads ~10 MB; subsequent loads hit the browser cache.

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
noise = rng.normal(scale=0.15, size=x.shape)
y = np.sin(x) + noise

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=4, alpha=0.6, label="noisy samples")
ax.plot(x, np.sin(x), color="tomato", lw=2, label="sin(x)")
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Noisy sine wave")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Try editing the cell — change `np.sin` to `np.cos`, or crank up the noise scale.

# Callout Notes

## Plain note

::: {.callout-note}
This is a plain, always-visible note callout.
It is useful for asides that every reader should see.
:::

## Collapsible tip

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
### Click to expand: numerical stability trick

When computing $\log\sum_i e^{x_i}$, subtract the max first:

$$\log\sum_i e^{x_i} = x_{\max} + \log\sum_i e^{x_i - x_{\max}}$$

This is the **log-sum-exp** trick and avoids floating-point overflow.[^lse]
:::

[^lse]: The same trick appears in softmax computation: divide numerator and denominator by $e^{x_{\max}}$, which leaves the result unchanged but keeps all exponents non-positive.

## Collapsible warning

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
### Click to expand: watch out for default RNG state

Calling `np.random.seed(42)` sets the *legacy* global RNG.
Prefer `np.random.default_rng(42)` which returns an isolated `Generator` object and does not touch global state.
:::

## Collapsible caution

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
### Click to expand: matplotlib backend

`plt.show()` behaves differently in scripts vs. notebooks vs. Pyodide.
In Pyodide the inline backend is active by default, so `plt.show()` renders the figure inline without extra setup.
:::

# Table

| Method | Variance | Bias | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naive MC | $O(N^{-1})$ | 0 | Baseline |
| Importance Sampling | $< O(N^{-1})$ | 0 | Optimal $q$ minimises variance |
| Control Variates | $< O(N^{-1})$ | 0 | Requires correlated $g$ |
| Quasi-MC | $O(N^{-2})$ | 0 | Low-discrepancy sequences |

# Blockquote

> The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers.
>
> — Richard Hamming

# Static Code

```python
def log_sum_exp(x):
    """Numerically stable log-sum-exp."""
    x_max = x.max()
    return x_max + np.log(np.exp(x - x_max).sum())
```

# Long Equation

The ELBO, log-marginal likelihood, and importance-weighted bound in one line:

$$\log p(y) = \log \int p(y \mid f)\,p(f)\,df \;\ge\; \mathbb{E}_{q(f)}\!\left[\log p(y \mid f)\right] - \mathrm{KL}\!\left[q(f)\,\|\,p(f)\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}_{q(f_i)}\!\left[\log p(y_i \mid f_i)\right] - \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{tr}(K^{-1}S) + (m-\mu)^\top K^{-1}(m-\mu) - d + \log|K| - \log|S|\right]$$

# More sidenotes

Monte Carlo integration estimates $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \frac{b-a}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x_i)$ where each $x_i \sim \text{Uniform}(a,b)$.[^mc]

[^mc]: Variance of the estimator is $\frac{(b-a)^2}{N}\text{Var}[f(X)]$, so the standard error shrinks as $O(N^{-1/2})$ regardless of dimension — this dimension-independence is the main appeal of Monte Carlo over deterministic quadrature in high dimensions.

The interactive cell below lets you experiment with sample count directly:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

def mc_integrate(f, a, b, n, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    xs = rng.uniform(a, b, n)
    return (b - a) * np.mean(f(xs))

n_samples = 10_000
estimate = mc_integrate(np.sin, 0, np.pi, n_samples)
exact = 2.0
print(f"Estimate ({n_samples:,} samples): {estimate:.6f}")
print(f"Exact:                          {exact:.6f}")
print(f"Error:                          {abs(estimate - exact):.2e}")
```
